user_goals_summary: Writes direct, reader-centered team updates where every line earns its place. Prioritizes acknowledging the real impact on the audience over justifying the sender's decisions. Values precise structural sequencing, clean endings, and specific accountability — rejects corporate platitudes, motivational closers, and vague group-asks.

rubric.name: Reader-Centered Framing
rubric.description: The message is framed around the reader's reality — their disruptions, their stakeholder pressures, their planning — rather than justifying the sender's reasoning or the company's position. Impact is named from the audience's side.

dimensions.label: Impact and stakes are described in terms of what changes for the reader, not why the sender made the decision
Avoids framing that explains or defends the sender's reasoning as the emotional center of the message
Names specific, concrete disruptions the reader is actually experiencing (not abstract sentiments)
Detail sections are framed as tools the reader can use, not status reports for the sender

rubric.name: Information Sequencing
rubric.description: The order of beats is deliberate: acknowledgment of the reader's situation comes before the news, and a human-facing impact line precedes any logistics or detail blocks. Each section lands because of what came before it.

dimensions.label: Acknowledgment of the reader's situation appears before the key news or change is delivered
A reader-facing impact line exists as its own beat before any logistics, bullet lists, or action items
Empathy and acknowledgment do not appear only after the news, where they read as cleanup or afterthought
The overall sequence has a clear arc — context, news, impact, details, next step — without doubling back

rubric.name: Clean, Decisive Endings
rubric.description: The message ends when the information ends. There is one clear next step or touchpoint, no competing actions, and no trailing sentiment or motivational closers.

dimensions.label: The close contains exactly one clear next step or touchpoint, not multiple competing actions
No motivational, sentimental, or 'all-hands' style closing lines after the final action
The email stops when there is nothing left to communicate — no trailing filler

rubric.name: Specific Accountability
rubric.description: People and owners are named specifically. Actions are directed to identifiable individuals, not generic placeholders or unnamed groups. Vague group-asks are replaced with clear ownership.

dimensions.label: Anyone responsible for a task or deliverable is identified by name, not by placeholder or role
Action items are directed to specific people, not phrased as 'everyone figure it out'
There is no unnecessary multiplication of contact points — if one owner covers it, don't add a second

rubric.name: Direct, Non-Corporate Tone
rubric.description: Tone is honest and human — direct without blaming, acknowledging without sugarcoating. Avoids language that reads like corporate communications, company all-hands, or PR framing.

dimensions.label: Avoids stock phrases like 'thank you for your resilience' or 'commitment to getting this right'
Delivers difficult information without assigning blame or softening it with false positivity
Empathy reads as genuine and specific, not as a performative gesture or obligatory acknowledgment
Uses plain, direct language rather than corporate or PR-style framing

rubric.name: Functional Density
rubric.description: Every line does identifiable work. Sentences are not decorative — each one advances the message, provides usable information, or serves a distinct structural purpose. If a line can be cut without loss, it should be.

dimensions.label: Each sentence has a clear function — no line exists purely for padding or transition
Each section or paragraph handles a distinct beat; no two sections do the same job
Information is stated once, clearly, without rephrasing or restating the same point

coaching_notes: This user iterates by subtraction — they approve structure quickly but spend most revisions cutting, reordering, and consolidating. Never ask 'do you want this warmer?' — they consistently remove warmth that feels performative. Their north star is: does every element serve the reader, and does the message end the moment the information does?

rubric.name: Information Sequencing
rubric.description: The order of beats is strict: acknowledge the situation first (one sentence max), then deliver the news, then explain why it matters from the reader's side, then one concrete next step. All elements can be present and the email still fails if they're in the wrong order.

dimensions.label: Acknowledgment of the reader's situation appears before the key news or change is delivered

The overall sequence has a clear arc — acknowledgment, news, reader-facing stakes, next step — without doubling back
Acknowledgment section is one sentence or less — does not expand into a full paragraph before delivering the news

rubric.name: Reader-Centered Framing
rubric.description: The message is framed around the reader's reality — their disruptions, their stakeholder pressures, their planning — rather than justifying the sender's reasoning or the company's position. The 'why this matters' section names what changes for the reader, not why the sender made the decision.

dimensions.label: Impact and stakes are described in terms of what changes for the reader, not why the sender made the decision
Avoids framing that explains or defends the sender's reasoning as the emotional center of the message
Names specific, concrete disruptions the reader is actually experiencing (not abstract sentiments)
Stakes are grounded in the reader's world — e.g. 'your team planned around this date' — not in the sender's intentions or values

rubric.name: Clean, Decisive Endings
rubric.description: The email ends at the action. One clear next step, no competing asks, and nothing after the CTA — no 'thanks for your patience,' no motivational lines, no trailing sentiment. Anything after the final action item weakens the close.

dimensions.label: The close contains exactly one clear next step or touchpoint, not multiple competing actions
No motivational, sentimental, or gratitude-based closing lines after the final action
The email stops when there is nothing left to communicate — no trailing filler
Closing line is forward-looking and action-oriented — it points to what happens next, not backward at effort already made

rubric.name: Specific Accountability
rubric.description: One clear owner per action, named directly in the message, with a deadline embedded in the action item itself. Group asks are non-actions. Action items should not over-specify org structure details the email has no business containing — ownership is clear without being presumptuous about how teams are organized. Deadlines are embedded per item — a single blanket deadline sentence before the list doubles information that should appear once, inline.

dimensions.label: Anyone responsible for a task or deliverable is identified by name or clear role, not left to a group to self-assign
Action items are directed to specific people — 'everyone please review' is not an action item
Each action item includes its own deadline or timeframe inline — not as a standalone summary sentence applied to all items at once
Action items do not over-specify org structure or process details the email shouldn't assume — ownership is clear without being presumptuous
There is no unnecessary multiplication of contact points — if one owner covers it, don't add a second

rubric.name: Functional Density
rubric.description: Every line does identifiable work. No padding, no decorative transitions, no restating the same point twice. Short paragraphs. If a sentence can be cut without losing information or structure, it should be cut.

dimensions.label: Each sentence has a clear function — no line exists purely for padding or transition
Each section or paragraph handles a distinct beat; no two sections do the same job
Information is stated once, clearly, without rephrasing or restating the same point
Paragraphs are short — dense blocks of text are broken up or cut

rubric.name: Direct, Non-Corporate Tone
rubric.description: Tone is honest and human — direct without blaming, professional without being stiff. No corporate platitudes, no PR framing, no excessive exclamation marks. Reads like a person wrote it, not a communications team.

dimensions.label: Avoids stock phrases like 'thank you for your resilience,' 'we remain committed,' or 'as we navigate this together'
Delivers difficult information without assigning blame or softening it with false positivity
Empathy reads as genuine and specific, not as a performative gesture or obligatory acknowledgment
Uses plain, direct language rather than corporate or PR-style framing
No excessive exclamation marks — enthusiasm does not substitute for substance

user_goals_summary: Writes direct, reader-centered team emails that acknowledge the real impact on recipients before delivering news or assignments. Values sharp stakes language that makes each reader feel the weight of their specific piece through implication — not description, blame, or abstract warnings. Ends emails cleanly at the action with no trailing sentiment.

rubric.name: Information Sequencing
rubric.description: The order of beats is strict: acknowledge the situation first (one sentence max), then deliver the news, then explain why it matters from the reader's side, then one concrete next step. All elements can be present and the email still fails if they're in the wrong order.

dimensions.label: Acknowledgment of the reader's situation appears before the key news or change is delivered
A reader-facing impact line exists as its own beat before any logistics, bullet lists, or action items
Empathy and acknowledgment do not appear only after the news, where they read as cleanup or afterthought
The overall sequence has a clear arc — acknowledgment, news, reader-facing stakes, next step — without doubling back
Acknowledgment section is one sentence or less — does not expand into a full paragraph before delivering the news

rubric.name: Reader-Centered Framing
rubric.description: The message is framed around the reader's reality — their disruptions, their downstream dependencies, their stakeholder pressures. The 'why this matters' section makes each reader picture what changes for them specifically, using implication rather than description. Stakes language creates felt weight, not abstract warnings.

dimensions.label: Avoids framing that explains or defends the sender's reasoning as the emotional center of the message
Stakes are grounded in the reader's world — their plans, their teams, their commitments — not in the sender's intentions or company values
Names specific, concrete disruptions the reader is actually experiencing — not abstract sentiments or general observations
Stakes make each individual reader picture their own downstream consequence — not a diffuse group warning like 'the whole team absorbs the delay'
Stakes create felt weight through implication — making the reader infer the consequence — rather than describing or explaining what will go wrong

rubric.name: Clean, Decisive Endings
rubric.description: The email ends at the action. One clear next step, no competing asks, and nothing after the CTA — no 'thanks for your patience,' no motivational lines, no trailing sentiment. Anything after the final action item weakens the close.

dimensions.label: The close contains exactly one clear next step or touchpoint, not multiple competing actions
No motivational, sentimental, or gratitude-based closing lines after the final action
The email stops when there is nothing left to communicate — no trailing filler
Closing line is forward-looking and action-oriented — it points to what happens next, not backward at effort already made

dimensions.label: Each sentence has a clear function — no line exists purely for padding or transition
Each section or paragraph handles a distinct beat; no two sections do the same job
Information is stated once, clearly, without rephrasing or restating the same point
Paragraphs are short — dense blocks of text are broken up or cut

rubric.name: Direct, Non-Corporate Tone
rubric.description: Tone is honest and human — direct without blaming, professional without being stiff. No corporate platitudes, no PR framing, no excessive exclamation marks. Accountability creates felt weight through consequence, not through accusation or finger-pointing.

dimensions.label: Avoids stock phrases like 'thank you for your resilience,' 'we remain committed,' or 'as we navigate this together'
Accountability language creates felt weight through consequence — not through accusation, finger-pointing, or 'that lands on you' phrasing
Empathy reads as genuine and specific, not as a performative gesture or obligatory acknowledgment
Uses plain, direct language rather than corporate or PR-style framing
No excessive exclamation marks — enthusiasm does not substitute for substance

coaching_notes: This user's non-negotiable is that stakes language must make each reader feel the weight of their own specific piece — abstract group warnings and described consequences consistently fail for them. They draw a sharp line between 'making someone feel the weight' (through implication) and 'pointing a finger' (through accusation); the former is the goal, the latter is a deal-breaker. When they say 'don't touch anything,' the draft is done — they iterate precisely on single elements and lock everything else early.

rubric.name: Clean, Decisive Endings
rubric.description: The email ends at the action. One clear next step, no competing asks, and nothing after the CTA — no 'thanks for your patience,' no motivational lines, no trailing sentiment. Anything after the final action item weakens the close. Concision does not override functional rationale attached to deadlines — a brief clause that explains why a deadline exists is load-bearing context, not filler.

dimensions.label: The close contains exactly one clear next step or touchpoint, not multiple competing actions
No motivational, sentimental, or gratitude-based closing lines after the final action

The email stops when there is nothing left to communicate — no trailing filler; but brief rationale attached to a deadline (e.g. 'so it doesn't push the window') is not filler — it's load-bearing context

Closing line is forward-looking and action-oriented — it points to what happens next, not backward at effort already made; a clause that connects an action to its consequence (e.g. 'so it doesn't push the window') is forward-looking — it names the outcome the action is meant to prevent
Closing rationale that explains why a deadline or action matters is not trailing content — only sentiment, gratitude, or motivation appearing after the final action counts as trailing filler to cut
